entry.title: Re: Stock transfer material aging report
entry.content:
Hi MohammedRiyaz,

The sending plant's GR date is not transferred to the receiving plant during a Stock Transfer process. Stock aging at the receiving plant is calculated based on the Goods Receipt posting date at the receiving plant, not on the original receipt date from the sending plant.

I hope this helps.

Best regards,

Thu Mai

entry.title: A Practical Approach to Automating External Parameter Changes in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

As SAP Integration Suite developers, we regularly need to adjust external parameter values throughout a project’s lifecycle. This is more than a good practice: it helps ensure that interfaces remain tenant-agnostic and easier to maintain across environments.

In many projects, these adjustments are only required during transport between environments. In more complex landscapes, however, what appears to be a simple task can quickly become a major operational challenge for any CPI specialist.

 

Example Scenario

Imagine you have built multiple interfaces and your project also includes SAP standard packages such as Cell and Gene Therapy Orchestration (CGTO) or SAP Digital Manufacturing (DM). In addition, SAP Event Mesh is being used as the event broker for CGTO.

Although this setup may appear straightforward, complications often arise at the architectural level. For reasons such as budget constraints or time pressure, the DEV and QA landscapes in this example include not only the standard systems but also hotfix systems dedicated to specific testing activities.

 

 

 

In the DEV scenario described above, SAP Integration Suite acts as the central backbone for all communication flows. While there may be two instances of SAP CGTO, two of SAP S/4HANA, and so on, there is only one SAP Integration Suite tenant responsible for connecting these systems correctly.

Once this landscape is defined, SAP Integration Suite must be adjusted accordingly to ensure that all communication channels function correctly. This becomes especially critical when the hotfix testing window is extremely limited and the parallel system remains offline. These adjustments can involve hundreds or even thousands of configuration values, making it unrealistic to update them manually within such a short period without a significant risk of error.

To illustrate the scale of the challenge, nearly 200 external parameters may be affected in a scenario like this, for example, SAP clients, queue paths, and system endpoints.

 

Solution Overview

A practical way to address this challenge is to leverage SAP Integration Suite itself, more specifically, the integration content available through SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

This approach makes it possible to extract the affected external parameters, maintain them in a spreadsheet, save them as a CSV file, and submit the update through an external tool such as Postman. The effort of preparing the configuration files is done only once; after that, switching between environment-specific configurations can be completed in just a few minutes by uploading the appropriate file.

 

Soft Configurations Framework

The concept is straightforward: this interface provides a controlled way to manage external parameters in SAP Integration Suite through an external client such as Postman.

 The goal is to reduce manual configuration effort across multiple environments while improving traceability and lowering the risk of human error.

 

How the Solution Works

Below are the iFlows included in this solution scope.

Get iFlows

This iFlow retrieves the external parameters of iFlows within the selected SAP Integration Suite packages and returns the result in either JSON or CSV format.

Options:

  • ignorePackages

Because of the large number of packages involved, this option was created to exclude any packages that fall outside the scope of the hotfix, for example, sandbox packages used for testing or other interfaces that are completely unrelated to the hotfix process.

  • returnPackage

If you only need to query a specific package, this option is much simpler, and significantly faster, than downloading every package available on the tenant.

  • Csv

By default, the interface returns the results in JSON format, which is convenient for inspection and integration. For SAP standard packages or large-scale parameter updates, however, CSV is often the most practical option because it allows all parameters to be reviewed and maintained efficiently in Microsoft Excel.

  • csvDelimiter

The user can also select the delimiter to be used in the CSV output.

 

Update Soft Config

This iFlow reads a JSON or CSV payload and updates the corresponding iFlows within the defined scope. It returns a full JSON report in Postman showing the affected external parameters, the previous and updated values, and the response status for each change.

Options:

  • Email

Because a full JSON report of the affected external parameters is generated, the interface can also send this report to a designated email inbox. This is particularly useful for traceability and audit purposes.

  • Deploy

This option allows the affected iflows to have their changes saved or saved and deployed.

 

iFlow Design and Processing Logic

 

Get All iFlows

Bellow are all the steps for this interface development.

Integration Process

  • Headers – Content Modifier: Reads all header parameters sent by a third-party application, such as Postman.
  • Read CSV Header – Groovy Script: Checks whether a CSV file has been requested.
  • Get Packages – Process Call: Invokes the local Get Packages process.

Get Packages

  • Get Packages – Request Reply: Retrieves all packages from SAP Integration Suite for the specified tenant (defined directly in the URL).
  • Read Packages – Groovy Script: Processes the returned payload and performs the following actions:
    • If returnPackage is enabled, filters the payload based on the packages specified in the client request.
    • If ignorePackages is enabled, returns all packages except those specified in the client request.
    • Creates a clean internal JSON structure containing the package ID, Name, and ShortText.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

def slim = results.collect { r ->
        [
            Id        : r?.Id ?: "",
            Name      : r?.Name ?: "",
            ShortText : r?.ShortText ?: ""
        ]
}
  • Total Packages – Router: If no packages are returned, the process terminates.
  • Get Integrations – Looping Process Call: Invokes the Get Artifacts from Package process for each package.

 

Get Artifacts from Package

  • Header – Content Modifier: Resets the current iFlow count.
  • Get Actual Package – Groovy Script: Since the next step requires the package ID for the request, this script retrieves the current package ID from the internal JSON message.
  • Get Artifacts from Package – Request Reply: Retrieves all iFlows associated with the selected package ID.
  • Get iFlows – Groovy Script: Processes the returned payload and appends the iFlow ID, Name, and ShortText, along with the current package ID, to the internal JSON message.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

found = false

jsonAll.each { item ->

	if ((item instanceof Map) && String.valueOf(item.Id) == targetPackage) 
	{
		item.Iflows = slim
		found = true
	}
}
  • Empty? – Router: If no iFlows are returned, the process ends and proceeds to the next package.
  • Get Soft Configurations – Looping Process Call: Invokes the local Get Soft Configurations process.

 

Get Soft configurations

  • Get iFlow – Groovy Script: For the package currently in scope, retrieves each iFlow ID required to request its soft configurations in the next step.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

if(resultValue)
{      
	message.setProperty("iflowID", resultValue)
	message.setProperty("currentCountIflow", targetIflowIx + 1)
}
  • Get iFlow Soft Configs – Request Reply: Retrieves all soft configurations for each iFlow.
  • Get Soft Configs and Update – Groovy Script: Processes the retrieved information by cleaning the message, filtering out soft configurations defined directly within the iFlow, and appending the remaining entries to the internal JSON message.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

Message processData(Message message) {

    def body       = message.getBody(String) ?: '{}'
    def parsed     = new JsonSlurper().parseText(body)
    def results    = parsed?.d?.results ?: []

    def slim = results.collect {
        [
            Parameter   : it?.ParameterKey   ?: "",
            Value       : it?.ParameterValue ?: ""
            //Description : it?.Description    ?: ""
        ]
    }

    ...

    def pkg = jsonAll.find { it?.Id == packageID }

    // Ignore list
    def ignoreList = ignore.split(",")
                        .collect { it.trim() }
                        .findAll { it }

    // remove from list
    slim = slim.findAll { r -> !(r?.Parameter in ignoreList) }    

    if (pkg?.Iflows) {
        // Find the iflow
        def flow = pkg.Iflows.find { it?.IdIflow == iflowID }
        if (flow) {
            flow.SoftConfig = slim
        }
    }
...
}

 

  Integration Process

  • Return Message – Content Modifier: Loads the internal JSON message into the message body.
  • isCSV? – Router: Checks whether a CSV output was requested. If not, the process ends and returns the internal JSON message.
  • CSV Convert – Groovy Script: Converts the JSON message into CSV format using the specified delimiter.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

// HEADER
    StringBuilder csv = new StringBuilder()
    csv.append(["PackageID","PackageName","IdIflow","NameIflow","Parameter","Value"].join(separator))
       .append(nl)

    // Clear escapes
    def esc = { v ->
        if (v == null) return ""
        def s = v.toString()
        def needsQuotes = s.contains(separator) || s.contains('"') || s.contains('\n') || s.contains('\r')
        s = s.replace('"', '""')
        needsQuotes ? "\"${s}\"" : s
    }

    // packages
    json.each { pkg ->
        def pkgId   = pkg.Id
        def pkgName = pkg.Name
        def iflows  = pkg.Iflows ?: []

        // iFlows
        iflows.each { iflow ->
            def iflowId   = iflow.IdIflow
            def iflowName = iflow.NameIflow
            def softCfgs  = iflow.SoftConfig ?: []

            // SoftConfig
            softCfgs.each { cfg ->
                def param = cfg.Parameter
                def value = cfg.Value

                csv.append([
                    esc(pkgId),
                    esc(pkgName),
                    esc(iflowId),
                    esc(iflowName),
                    esc(param),
                    esc(value)
                ].join(separator)).append(nl)
            }
        }
    }
  • Header – Content Modifier: Updates the Content-Type header to text/csv.
  • End: Terminates the process.

 

Update Soft Configurations

Bellow are all the steps for this interface development.

Integration Process

  • Count – Content Modifier: Reads all configurations and header parameters sent by a third-party application, such as Postman.
  • Validate JSON Message – Groovy Script: Checks whether the incoming message is in JSON or CSV format, validates its structure, and transforms the payload into an internal JSON message.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

Message processData(Message message) {

    ...
        
    // is JSON?
    try {
        parsed = slurper.parseText(body)
        if (parsed instanceof Map || parsed instanceof List) {
            isJson = true
        }
    } catch (Exception e) {
        isJson = false
    }

    // if its json, end
    if (!isJson) 
    {
        ...
    }
    else
    {
        /////////////////////////////////////////
        //json
        /////////////////////////////////////////
        
        try 
        {
            json = new JsonSlurper().parseText(body)
        } catch (e) {
            error = "Invalid JSON format."
        }

        // array?
        if (!(json instanceof List)) {
            error = "JSON must be an array at root level."
        }

        json.eachWithIndex { item, idx ->

            // IdIflow
            if (!item?.IdIflow || !(item.IdIflow instanceof String)) {
                error = "Element at index ${idx} missing valid 'IdIflow' (string)."
            }

            // SoftConfig
            if (!(item?.SoftConfig instanceof List)) {
                error = "Element '${item.IdIflow}' missing valid 'SoftConfig' array."
            }

            // config
            item.SoftConfig.eachWithIndex { cfg, cidx ->
                if (!cfg?.Parameter || !(cfg.Parameter instanceof String)) {
                    error = "Invalid 'Parameter' in '${item.IdIflow}' at SoftConfig index ${cidx}."
                }
                if (cfg.Value == null) { // allowed empty, but not null
                    error = "Missing 'Value' in '${item.IdIflow}' at SoftConfig index ${cidx}."
                }
            }
        }
    }
    def deploy = properties.get("deploy")
        
    if(!deploy)
    {
        message.setProperty("deploy", 'NO')
    }
    else
    {
        if( deploy.toUpperCase() != 'YES')
            message.setProperty("deploy", 'NO')
        else
            message.setProperty("deploy", 'YES')        
    }
...
}

 

  • All Good? – Router: Verifies whether the message is valid. If validation fails, the process ends and returns an error message.
  • iFlow – Looping Process Call: Invokes the local iFlow Logic process.

 

Iflow Logic

  • Get iFlow – Groovy Script: Reads information for each iFlow from the internal payload.
  • Get SoftConfigs – Request Reply: Retrieves the existing soft configurations for the iFlow currently in scope.
  • Store Previous Configs – Content Modifier: Stores the response payload for later use.
  • Fetch Token – Process Call: Invokes the Fetch Token local process.

 

Fetch Token

  • Header – Content Modifier: Configures the request to fetch the X-CSRF token header required for the next call.
  • Fetch Token – Request Reply: Retrieves the token through a GET request.
  • End: Terminates the local process and returns control to the iFlow Logic process call. 

 

 Iflow Logic

  • Soft Configs – Looping Process Call: Iteratively invokes the local Soft Configs process for each configuration item.

 

Soft Configs

  • Get Soft Config – Groovy Script: Processes each soft parameter individually for the iFlow currently in scope.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

if (configIndex < 0 || configIndex >= obj.SoftConfig.size()) 
{
   message.setProperty("currentParameter", "")
   message.setProperty("currentParameterValue", "")
}
else
{
   def value     = obj.SoftConfig[configIndex]?.Value?.toString()
   def parameter = obj.SoftConfig[configIndex]?.Parameter?.toString()

   message.setProperty("currentParameterValue", value)
   message.setProperty("currentParameter", parameter)
}
  • Prepare Body – Content Modifier: Configures the request body in the expected format for the next call and sets the required CSRF token headers.
  • Update Config – Request Reply: Updates the current soft parameter through a PUT request.
  • Read Response – Groovy Script: Reads the HTTP response code and message, while also retrieving the previous value that was cached earlier in the process.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

Message processData(Message message) {

    def body         = message.getBody(String)
    def jsonPrevious = message.getProperty("jsonPrevious")?.toString()
    def parameterKey = message.getProperty("currentParameter")?.toString()
    def head         = message.getHeaders();
    def httpCode     = head.get("CamelHttpResponseCode");

    def json         = new JsonSlurper().parseText(jsonPrevious)

    def results      = json?.d?.results
    def node         = results.find { it?.ParameterKey == parameterKey }
    def value        = node?.ParameterValue?.toString()

    message.setProperty("beforeParameterValue", value)
    message.setProperty("responseMessage", body)
    message.setProperty("responseCode", httpCode)

    return message
}
  • JSON Final Message – Groovy Script: Updates the internal JSON message with the new values obtained from the previous step.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

def newCfg = [
        Parameter      : parameter,
        ValueBefore    : valueBefore,
        ValueUpdated   : valueUpdated,
        ResponseCode   : responseCode,
        ResponseMessage: responseMsg
]

def existingIdx = node.SoftConfig.findIndexOf { it?.Parameter == parameter }

if (existingIdx >= 0) 
{
   node.SoftConfig[existingIdx] = newCfg
} 
else 
{
   node.SoftConfig << newCfg
}
  • End: Once the loop finishes, control returns to the iFlow Logic process and execution continues.

 

iFlow Logic

  • Deploy? – Router: Checks whether deployment was requested by the user.
  • Deploy – Request Reply: Sends a POST request using the iFlow ID currently in scope to trigger deployment.
  • Update JSON – Groovy Script: Updates the internal JSON message with the deployment result.
  • End: If no additional iFlows remain, control returns to the Integration Process.

 

Integration Process

  • Final Payload – Content Modifier: Loads the internal JSON message into the message body.
  • Monitor – Groovy Script: Attaches the payload to the MPL.
// Part of the script 
// Full script can be found on the link of the bottom of the article

if (messageLog != null) 
{
   messageLog.addAttachmentAsString('Soft config report', body, 'text/plain')
}
  • Has Email – Router: Checks whether an email request was provided. If not, the process ends.
  • Send Email – Send: Sends an email with the payload attached.

 

Iflow configuration

Get all iflows

Receiver

ParameterValue
ReceiverGet_Soft_Configurations
Adapter TypeHTTP
Addresshttps://[ CPI tenant ]/api/v1/IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts(Id='${property.iflowID}',Version='active')/Configurations
Proxy TypeInternet

AuthenticationBasic
Credential Name[cpi credential]
Request HeadersAccept

 

ParameterValue
ReceiverGet_Iflows
Adapter TypeHTTP
Addresshttps://[ CPI tenant ] /api/v1/IntegrationPackages('${property.packageID}')/IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts
Proxy TypeInternet
MethodGet
AuthenticationBasic
Credential Name[cpi credential]
Request HeadersAccept

 

ParameterValue
ReceiverGet_Packages
Adapter TypeHTTP
Addresshttps://[ CPI tenant ] /api/v1/IntegrationPackages
Proxy TypeInternet
MethodGet
AuthenticationBasic
Credential Name[cpi credential]
Request HeadersAccept

More

ParameterValue
Change1Test 1
Change 2Test 2
Change 3Test 3
IgnoreParameterSAP_ProfileID

Change1-3: Dummy external parameters for testing. Have no effect on the iflow. 

IgnoreParameter: Add parameters that should not be returned on the response.

 

Change Soft Configs

Receiver

ParameterValue
ReceiverDeploy
Adapter TypeHTTP
Addresshttps://[ CPI tenant ] /api/v1/DeployIntegrationDesigntimeArtifact?Id='${property.IdIflowExtracted}'&Version='active'
Proxy TypeInternet
MethodPOST
AuthenticationBasic
Credential Name[cpi credential]
Request HeadersAccept, X-CSRF-Token, Cookie

 

ParameterValue
ReceiverGetToken
Adapter TypeHTTP
Addresshttps://[ CPI tenant ] /api/v1/IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts(Id='Get_all_iflows',Version='active')/Configurations
Proxy TypeInternet
MethodGET
AuthenticationBasic
Credential Name[cpi credential]
Request HeadersAccept, X-CSRF-Token

 

ParameterValue
ReceiverUpdateSoftConfigs
Adapter TypeHTTP
Addresshttps://[ CPI tenant ] /api/v1/IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts(Id='${property.IdIflowExtracted}',Version='active')/$links/Configurations('${property.currentParameter}')
Proxy TypeInternet
MethodPUT
AuthenticationBasic
Credential Name
[cpi credential]
Request HeadersAccept, X-CSRF-Token, Cookie

 

ParameterValue
ReceiverGetSoftConfigs

Addresshttps://[ CPI tenant ] /api/v1/IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts(Id='${property.IdIflowExtracted}',Version='active')/Configurations

Request HeadersAccept

[email protection]

[email from]
To${property.userEmail}

More

Tenant[cpi tenant]

Tenant: used for the email subject only.

 

Example of a call through Postman

In this case, I’m returning the SoftConfigurations package.

JSON file format

CSV, with ; as delimiter

 

Now, let us move forward with the following change:

[
    {
        "IdIflow": "Get_all_iflows",
        "SoftConfig": [
            {
                "Parameter": "change1",
                "Value": "It works from Postman 1"
            },
            {
                "Parameter": "change2",
                "Value": "It works from Postman 2"
            }
        ]
    }
]

Call Change Soft Configs using the payload above, requesting both deployment and email notification.

Email report

And the attachment

[
    {
        "IdIflow": "Get_all_iflows",
        "Deploy": {
            "Required": "YES",
            "ResponseCode": 202,
            "ID": "---"
        },
        "SoftConfig": [
            {
                "Parameter": "change1",
                "ValueBefore": "Test 1",
                "ValueUpdated": "It works from Postman 1",
                "ResponseCode": "202",
                "ResponseMessage": ""
            },
            {
                "Parameter": "change2",
                "ValueBefore": "Test 2",
                "ValueUpdated": "It works from Postman 2",
                "ResponseCode": "202",
                "ResponseMessage": ""
            }
        ]
    }
]

If I go to the iflow configuration, I can see that the update was done.

Applicability

 

Interface Connection Management

This was the primary motivation for creating the interface. Even with strong controls in place, manual configuration always carries some risk. Storing configurations in separate files not only saves time, but also provides a more reliable and resilient operating model. Teams can maintain environment-specific configurations independently and apply them when needed.

 

Technical Documentation

Documenting external parameters and their values is rarely a pleasant task. With this interface, parameters can be exported as a CSV file, refined in Microsoft Excel, and then reused in technical documentation with far less manual effort.

 

Configurations After Transport to Another Environment

From a governance perspective, managing critical parameters in a controlled format can be preferable to maintaining them directly in a web interface. CSV files reduce the risk of mistakes, especially in scenarios where SAP CTMS transports interfaces with incorrect external parameters.

They also make it easier to identify patterns and provide a broader, more structured view of the configuration landscape.

 

Final thoughts

I created this tool to address a highly time-consuming challenge in day-to-day delivery work. As a result, it was designed with enough flexibility to evolve and support additional use cases over time. Potential enhancements could include AI-based support for technical documentation or simplified access for non-technical colleagues and clients who need a clearer view of active integrations.

Unfortunately, time, or more precisely, the lack of it, has been the main constraint preventing further development of these additional capabilities.

I hope you find this tool as useful as I found the process of creating it. There are, of course, many ways to solve the same problem; this is simply one approach that I enjoyed designing and implementing.

 

Groovy scripts

All groovy scripts found on this article can be downloaded from the following GitHub page.

Sap CPI softConfigs

entry.title: SAP Convergent Charging 2022.2 updater becomes slow/unresponsive under master data pressure
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are investigating a performance/stability issue in SAP Convergent Charging 2022.2 where the updater becomes very slow or temporarily unresponsive when pressure is applied through Web Service-based master data and provider contract operations.

Context

Landscape:

  • SAP Convergent Charging 2022.2
  • SAP CRM + SAP ERP-based BRIM integration
  • Oracle database
  • SAP CC updater used for master data/subscriber account / charging contract provisioning
  • Direct updater Web Service URL tested, without Load Balancer
  • The second updater is shut down and not part of any target group
  • Therefore, passive-updater routing and load-balancer routing are currently out of scope

Functional scenario:

  • External/client-side process creates master data and contracts in CRM
  • Business partners, contract accounts, and provider contracts are created/changed
  • These objects should be replicated/provisioned into SAP CC through the updater
  • Under pressure, the updater becomes slow and sometimes unresponsive
  • Even a simple Web Service ping/WSDL check against the direct updater URL can take more than 10 seconds, sometimes around 20 seconds

Observed behaviour

When there is no pressure:

  • Direct updater Web Service responds normally
  • Ping/WSDL checks can be fast
  • The updater appears healthy

When pressure is applied:

  • Contract/master-data creation or change operations quickly slow down
  • Updater Web Service response time suddenly increases
  • Ping/WSDL calls to the same direct updater URL start taking 10–20 seconds
  • The updater appears alive but slow/unresponsive
  • This looks like the updater Web Service processing path is blocked or saturated

What we already ruled out

  1. Load Balancer
  • The issue was reproduced using the direct updater URL
  • The second updater is shut down and not in the target group
  • Therefore, this is not caused by ALB routing to a passive updater
  1. OS limits
    The running updater process has inherited the expected limits:

Max processes:
506949

Max open files:
1048576

Max locked memory:
unlimited

  1. HugePages
    Host HugePages:

HugePages_Total: 34500
HugePages_Free: 31387
HugePages_Rsvd: 1116
Hugepagesize: 2048 kB
Hugetlb: 70656000 kB

Updater process HugePages:

Private_Hugetlb: 6375424 kB
HugetlbPages: 6375424 kB
VmSwap: 0 kB
Threads: 112
VmRSS: 667600 kB

Interpretation:

  • HugePages are active
  • HugePages are not exhausted
  • The updater is not swapping
  • Process/thread/file limits do not look like the cause
  1. JVM/GC/host pressure from previous measurements
    Previous load windows did not show:
  • Full GC pressure
  • Heap exhaustion
  • Swap usage
  • CPU saturation
  • Local disk saturation

Current updater-relevant parameters

Updater Web Service / SQL / diagnostics settings:

WS_SERVER_THREAD_COUNT = 16
WS_SERVER_QUEUE_SIZE = computed
SQLHELPER_CONNECTION_COUNT = 64
SQLHELPER_DB_TIMEOUT = 0
SQLHELPER_RECONNECT_ATTEMPTS = 5
SQLHELPER_RECONNECT_INTERVAL = 10
SQLHELPER_STATEMENT_CACHE_SIZE = 1

THREAD_DUMP_ENABLED = false
THREAD_DUMP_THRESHOLD_SQL_TIME = 0
THREAD_DUMP_TRIGGER_PERIOD = 0
THREAD_DUMP_OUTPUT_PATH = work/dump
THREAD_DUMP_MINIMUM_INTERVAL = 60

Audit is enabled:

AUDIT_ACTIVATION = true
AUDIT_OBJECT_ACTIVATION = true
AUDIT_DOMAIN = CATALOG, CUSTOMER, TECHNICAL, ADMINISTRATION
AUDIT_OBJECT_DOMAIN = CATALOG,CATALOG_TABLE_DATA,CUSTOMER,CUSTOMER_TABLE_DATA,TECHNICAL

Current hypothesis

Because the direct updater Web Service URL becomes slow under pressure, and OS/HugePages/GC/CPU/disk do not show clear pressure, we suspect one of the following:

  1. All updater Web Service worker threads become occupied
  2. WS requests wait in the updater Web Service queue
  3. WS workers are blocked on Oracle DB wait / DB lock / slow SQL / SQL reconnect
  4. Audit/object-audit database writes are slowing down provisioning operations
  5. Internal updater lock contention occurs inside the Java/SAP CC runtime
  6. A small number of long-running provisioning calls block the available Web Service worker pool, causing even simple ping/WSDL requests to wait

Questions

  1. In SAP CC 2022.2, is it expected that if all updater Web Service worker threads are occupied, even simple Web Service ping/WSDL calls become slow because they wait behind the same Web Service worker/queue mechanism?
  2. For CRM/ERP-driven master data and provider contract provisioning, which SAP CC updater indicators are the most useful to prove Web Service worker saturation or queueing?
  3. Is there a recommended way to monitor the active usage of WS_SERVER_THREAD_COUNT and WS_SERVER_QUEUE_SIZE on the updater?
  4. Could AUDIT_ACTIVATION=true and AUDIT_OBJECT_ACTIVATION=true significantly slow down mass creation/change of customer master data and provider contracts in the updater?
  5. Which database tables or Oracle waits should be checked first for updater provisioning pressure, especially around audit/object snapshots and provider contract creation?
  6. Is SQLHELPER_DB_TIMEOUT=0 acceptable for this type of high-volume provisioning scenario, or should DB connectivity/SQL wait detection be configured differently?
  7. Is SQLHELPER_STATEMENT_CACHE_SIZE=1 a known bottleneck for updater-heavy provisioning workloads, or should this only be changed under SAP guidance?

  8. For this symptom, the correct next step is to enable:

    • THREAD_DUMP_ENABLED=true
    • THREAD_DUMP_THRESHOLD_SQL_TIME=5000
    • THREAD_DUMP_MINIMUM_INTERVAL=60
    • THREAD_DUMP_OUTPUT_PATH=work/dump
    • THREAD_DUMP_TRIGGER_PERIOD=0

    Does SAP CC generate a thread dump when an SQL query exceeds 5 seconds?

  9. In addition to automatic thread dumps, should we also capture manual jstack dumps exactly when the updater Web Service ping takes 10–20 seconds?
  10. Are there known SAP Notes, configuration recommendations, or best practices for updater pressure during CRM/ERP master data replication and provider contract provisioning in SAP CC 2022.2?

The evidence we plan to collect during the next slowdown

At the exact moment when the updater Web Service ping takes 10–20 seconds:

Admin+:

server status updater
get SERVICES_STATISTICS_MEMORY updater
get WS_SERVER_THREAD_COUNT MEMORY updater
get WS_SERVER_QUEUE_SIZE MEMORY updater
get SQLHELPER_CONNECTION_COUNT MEMORY updater
get FREE_MEMORY MEMORY updater
get TOTAL_MEMORY MEMORY updater

OS/JVM:

cat /proc/<updater_pid>/status
cat /proc/<updater_pid>/limits
cat /proc/<updater_pid>/smaps_rollup
jstack -l <updater_pid>

Socket/file evidence:

ss -tanp
lsof -p <updater_pid>

Oracle:

v$session wait events
blocking sessions
long-running SQL
top SQL by elapsed time
audit/object audit table pressure

The main question is:

What is the correct SAP CC way to prove whether the updater collapse is caused by Web Service thread saturation, DB wait/lock, audit/object-audit persistence, SQL reconnect/wait, or internal Java/updater lock contention?
SAP Convergent Charging

entry.title: Architecting for Autonomy: SAP Sapphire Madrid Takeaways
entry.content:
The SAP Autonomous Enterprise is an AI-driven operating model where AI agents and assistants execute end-to-end business workflows independently. Humans set the strategic direction while AI manages mission-critical processes within built-in governance, security, and compliance guardrails.

This was the strategic theme in the keynote of SAP Sapphire Madrid 2026 - a paradigm shift from Digital transformation to something more ambitious - Autonomous Enterprise.

 

The first step to achieving this ambition, as per SAP, is to lay the groundwork with Business Data Fabric combined with Knowledge Graphs and Company memory, thus establishing a strong foundation of governance and data with context and relation.

This foundation of contextual intelligence is further strengthened by adhering to Clean Core principles with BTP extensibility capabilities.

How can organisations achieve this ambitious goal?

 

Establish "Clean Core with BTP extensibility" as an architecture guideline

Organisations can reap the benefits of moving to S/4 HANA by adopting out-of-the-box features for standard processes and adapting specific requirements using BTP extensibility. This serves as a critical criteria to form a strong data foundation layer. This data foundation can then be enhanced with data from other processes and non-SAP sources through the SAP Business Data Fabric

From Data to Intelligence

Data can be only as useful as the context and relation it has to the processes of the organisation. SAP Knowledge Graphs help in forming these connections between business processes and datasets. This is further aided by Company Memory which captures historical decisions and patterns

Process + AI

Organisations can benefit from migrating from legacy business processes and moving to best practices. Gaps in process can be identified by using process mining tools complimented with SAP Signavio to close these gaps.

After establishing a lean process, SAP recommends using SAP Business AI and Joule to enable intelligent automation across functions such as HR, finance, procurement etc.

Governance Frameworks

One of the key steps to achieving this ambition is to setup a Governance framework where experts define and implement ethics, guardrails and policies on the outcomes of these AI solutions

The journey to transforming into an Autonomous Enterprise is a tough but attainable goal powered by leadership support.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 03 - Declarative field control with @mandatory
SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model: What’s New as of 2026-06-01?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!

Welcome to this month’s edition of our What’s New series for SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model (SAP PaPM Cloud SM). We’re excited to share the latest enhancements and improvements , continuing our commitment to improving usability, performance, and the overall user experience.

Let’s dive in! 🚀

System

Application Monitor | Updated Run Type Labels

The run type labels in the Application Monitor have been updated with clearer, more descriptive terms, making it easier for users to identify and review log statuses. For a complete reference to all updated run types, you may check our SAP Help Portal for Event Status and Run Types.

The updated labels are also reflected in the Process Monitor.

Process Monitor | Cancel Queued Tasks

We’ve improved task management in the Process Monitor- you can now cancel tasks that are still waiting in the queue, not just tasks that are already running. Previously, you could only terminate tasks that were actively running. If a task was queued and waiting to run, there was no way to stop it without waiting for it to start first. 

With this update, you can now cancel queued tasks directly from the Process Monitor screen — giving you more control over your workload without unnecessary waiting. 

How it works:

  1. Initiate two or more large functions at the same time so that one enters a Queued status. 
  2. In the Process Monitor (Execution Log), locate the task with a Queued status. Hover over the status icon to confirm.
  3. Select the queued task row then choose the Cancel Process button to initiate the cancellation. 

  4. Confirmation messages appear: two notification banners will appear at the bottom of the screen: 

    • A blue banner: "Canceling process" — indicating the cancellation is in progress 
    • A green banner: "Process in queue canceled" — confirming the task was successfully removed
  5. Task status updates to "Canceled". Once canceled, the task status icon updates in the list. Hover over the icon to see the "Canceled" tooltip confirming the task is no longer queued. 

Administration

Users

The Users application now includes a Status column, allowing administrators to quickly identify whether a user is active or inactive. Administrators can also export this data, including the Status column, to a CSV or XLSX file

To view this new feature, perform the following steps:

  1. Go to Menu > Administration. 
  2. Select Users.

The Status column by default is placed at the right-most part of the User Management screen.



For more information about the User Management screen, visit our SAP Help Portal. 

Content Management | Notifications

With the latest enhancement, users are now notified via in-app or email alerts whenever authorized teams are assigned or unassigned during the import or overwriting of private environments in the Content Management screen. Notifications are also sent when the authorized team remains unchanged, ensuring members are informed if their environment has been overwritten. 

Sample In-App Notifications 

Sample Email Notifications 

To learn more about notifications, you may visit the SAP Help documentation and the SAP Community blog post on Global Notification Settings. 

Settings | Optional Features | New Implementation of Remote Function Adapter Python Available for All

As previously announced in our blog post, version 2 of the Remote Function Adapter (RFA) Python function type implementation, initially introduced as an optional feature, is now the default implementation in all productive tenants using the SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model application

This new implementation includes an upgraded platform, Python runtime 3.13.2, and updated supported Python library versions. 

We hope you were able to test the new implementation during its optional phase and ensure that your Python scripts continue to run smoothly and deliver the expected results.  

However, if you did not have enough time to validate your models, or if you identified compatibility issues, a rollback option is now available as an optional feature for a limited time. With this option, administrators can temporarily revert to the previous RFA Python implementation while modelers complete their adjustments. 

For more information, see Optional Features Settings on SAP Help Portal.  

Note for Modelers:
If your models use the RFA Python function type and you have not yet validated your Python scripts against the updated Python runtime and library versions introduced with RFA Python version 2, before it became a default one, you can contact your administrator and activate the rollback option. This will give you additional time to test, adjust, and confirm that your scripts are fully compatible. However, please be informed that this rollback is offered only for a limited time. 

Modeling Screen

Extended Data Length Support for DEC, CURR, and QUAN Fields in OData Model View 
SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model now supports extended data lengths for DEC (Decimal), CURR (Currency), and QUAN (Quantity) environment fields. You can create and edit these key figure fields with a data length of up to 38 characters and map them to OData source fields with data lengths ranging from 32 to 38 characters. 

This enhancement aligns SAP PaPM Cloud SM with the higher precision standards used by SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) and other OData providers, enabling seamless integration of high-precision numeric data. 

As a result, organizations using SAC or similar OData-based sources can directly map high-precision decimal, currency, and quantity fields—without the need to restructure or transform source data. This eliminates a key integration limitation, reduces data preparation effort, and accelerates time-to-value when building SAP PaPM Cloud SM models. 

What Has Changed 
Previously, SAP PaPM Cloud SM supported a maximum data length of 31 characters for DEC, CURR, and QUAN fields. This limitation created an incompatibility with source systems that exposed higher-precision fields (32+ characters), preventing these fields from being selected during OData mapping. 

With this update: 

  • Field Creation and Editing 
    You can now define DEC, CURR, and QUAN environment fields with a data length between 1 and 38 characters. 
  • OData Field Mapping 
    Source fields with data lengths of 32 to 38 characters are now available for selection in the mapping configuration. 
  • Backward Compatibility 
    Existing fields with a data length of 31 characters or below remain unchanged, and all existing mappings continue to work. 

Note: This enhancement applies only to DEC, CURR, and QUAN data types. Other data types are not affected. 

Scenario 1: Automatic Field Mapping Using “Map Selected” 

Use this option to automatically create and map environment fields from OData source fields- including those with extended data lengths. 

  1. In your environment,  configure a Model View → OData function. 
  2. Maintain the OData source connection via Connection Name. 
  3. Expand the Fields node. 
  4. Select the source fields to include. Fields with DEC, CURR, or QUAN data types, and data length up to 38 characters are now available. 
  5. Choose Map Selected. 
  6. The system automatically creates corresponding environment fields with matching data types and lengths (up to 38 characters) and maps them. 
  7. Choose Save. 

Note: Source fields with data length greater than 38 characters are not supported and will not be included in automatic mapping. 

Scenario 2: Manual Field Mapping with Extended Data Length 

Use this option when mapping fields individually. 

  1. In your environment, configure a Model View → OData function. 
  2. Maintain the OData source connection via Connection Name. 
  3. Expand the Fields node. 
  4. Ensure the target environment field is defined as DEC, CURR, or QUAN with a data length that matches or exceeds the source (up to 38 characters). Update the field if necessary. 
  5. In the source field dropdown, select the desired field. Fields with data length up to 38 characters are now available. 
  6. Map the source field to the corresponding environment field. 
  7. Choose Save. 

Note: If the environment field has a smaller data length than the source field, it will not appear in the selection list. Update the field length before mapping. 

For more details on configuring environment fields and data lengths, refer to the Data Types, Master and Hierarchy Data | SAP Help Portal. 

SOAP Writer | Model Table SOAP
The Model Table SOAP function- an underlying component of the SOAP Writer, which enables posting financial data from SAP PaPM Cloud SM to SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud- now includes a Transport Data option. This enhancement allows Model Table SOAP data to be included across different transport and reuse scenarios. 

For more information about the SOAP Writer, see our dedicated blog post. 

By selecting the Transport Data option, the corresponding Model Table SOAP data is included during environment export, environment copy, and when copying and pasting functions, ensuring that the data is consistently available and reused across all these operations. 

Joule

AI Enhancements

Several new capabilities have been introduced to Joule in the latest release, expanding what you can do from your reports.
What's new: 

  • Animations – Joule now includes transition animations throughout the panel. The option to turn off animations is accessible from the Settings item at the bottom of the History sidebar. 
  • AI Notice – A new AI Notice item is now accessible from the bottom of the History sidebar, providing an AI disclosure reminder and a link to Joule Data Protection and Privacy information. 
  • File Upload – You can now attach files directly to your Joule conversation using the attachment icon or drag-and-drop. Joule reads the file content and uses it as additional context when answering questions. Supported formats are CSV, XLSX, and PDF. Up to 5 files can be attached per conversation, with a maximum of 5 MB per file. 
  • Web Search – When enabled by an administrator, Joule can now search the internet to answer general questions beyond your report's data. Results are cited inline, and a Sources panel lists the numbered web citations as clickable links. 
  • Inline Report Editor – A new inline editing capability is available when working in Edit mode on a report. Select any text paragraph, enter an editing prompt (e.g., "Rewrite this more formally"), and Joule rewrites the selected text based on your instructions. 

For a detailed explanation of each feature and a complete overview of all Joule capabilities, see our blog post: SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model: Joule AI - Features and Capabilities in Report Management.

That’s all for this update! 🎁

We hope this release helps further enhance your experience with SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model. To learn more about the topics covered in this post, explore the links provided throughout the blog and refer to the SAP Help Portal for detailed documentation.

Stay tuned- more exciting updates are on the way!

entry.title: Lack of History Tracking for Key Figure Changes in Object Store and HANA DB (SAP Datasphere)
entry.content:
We are working on a data model in SAP Datasphere where business data is being ingested into both:

  • Object Store (Data Lake / Files)
  • SAP HANA Cloud (local tables / persisted models)

In our use case, key figures (measures) are subject to updates and corrections during data submissions and transformations.

However, we observe that:

  • Changes to key figures are overwritten
  • There is no standard history tracking/versioning mechanism
  • This limitation exists in both:
    • Object Store-based datasets
    • HANA DB persisted tables inside Datasphere

entry.title: ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code
entry.content:
Introduction

ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code provides set of tools for ABAP development within the ABAP Cloud development model. 

Follow below steps to access VS Code and Establish system connection
1. Download Visual Studio Code
2. Installing ABAP Development Tools for VS Code
3. Establishing a System Connection via HTTP Destination
      Open Destination Settings
      Add Destination to Workspace
      Add Package to Workspace

Download Visual Studio Code

To download Visual studio Code, please navigate to below URL.

Download Visual Studio Code - Free AI Code Editor for Mac, Linux, Windows

Installing ABAP Development Tools for VS Code

Navigate to the Extensions tab in the Activity Bar, search for ABAP Development Tools, Install the extension and restart the IDE.

Installation is done.

Establishing a System Connection via HTTP Destination

Creation of a destination is needed to establish a system connection between frontend and backend via HTTP.

Shortcut key - Ctrl + Shift + P to open the Command Palette.

In the Command Palette, choose ABAP: New Destination.

Between the two connection types displayed, choose HTTP.

  • RFC for on-premise or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
  • HTTP for SAP BTP ABAP environment or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Enter the URL of the cloud system.

Enter a system ID

The system connection has been established and is visible under Workspace in the Explorer view.

Open Destination Settings

In the Command Palette, search ABAP: Open Destination Settings (JSON)

Destination Settings

Add Destination to Workspace

In the Command Palette, search ABAP: Add Destination as Folder to Workspace

Destination is added as folder to workspace

Add Package to Workspace

In the Command Palette, search ABAP: Add Package as Folder to Workspace

Select a destination

Select Open with Default Browser or copy to clipboard

Logged successfully.

Search package

Package is added to workspace

Data Definition from that package can be opened.

Data Definition details

Opening Development Object

In the Command Palette, search ABAP: Open Object

Shortcut key - Ctrl + Shift + A to open object.

Search object

Object is opened.

Creating ABAP Development Objects

Create New ABAP Object

Ctrl + Shift + P to open the Command Palette.

In the Command Palette, search ABAP: Create New ABAP Object or choose short cut key Ctrl + Shift + Alt + N.

Select the relevant object type.

Choose Data definition

Enter or select the Package Name, the Name, and the Description.

Choose package

Enter Name of object

Enter description of object

Enter reference object

Added all fields of source object

Checking ABAP Syntax

The ABAP syntax check is enabled to verify code-based development objects before activating.

In the Command Palette, search ABAP: Check Object.

Shortcut key - Ctrl + Shift + P to check syntax

Activating ABAP Development Objects

In the Command Palette, search ABAP: Activate.

Shortcut key - Ctrl + F3 to activate the object.

Activated successfully.

In the Command Palette, search ABAP: Activate All.

Shortcut key - Ctrl + Shift + F3 to activate the objects.

Keyboard Shortcuts for ABAP Development

 

Conclusion

ABAP development tools provides ABAP development to the preferred editor, enabled to create, edit, debug, and manage ABAP artifacts directly in VS Code. 

It support object types like below

  • ABAP core objects (classes, interfaces)
  • Core Data Services (CDS data definitions, CDS metadata extensions, CDS entity extension, CDS types, CDS aspects, CDS access controls)
  • ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (service bindings, service definitions, behavior definitions, change documents, number range objects)

ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code provide features that may depend on the client or require a specific backend system version

 

References

  • ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code | SAP Help Portal
  • Installing ABAP Development Tools for VS Code | SAP Help Portal
  • Creating ABAP Development Objects | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Re: corsfilter on /medias
entry.content:
Dear @phoude ,

The /medias endpoint is handled by the mediaweb extension (web application), which has its own separate CORS filter configuration. Without explicit CORS properties for mediaweb, no Access-Control-Allow-Origin header is returned for media requests, causing the browser to block them.

we request to maintain separate cors filter for medias further and do test in local or lower environment first.

corsfilter.mediaweb.

 

Regards,

Prasanth

SAP Support

entry.title: Improving SAP HANA DB Performance in S/4HANA: Two Common CDS View Pitfalls and How to Fix Them
entry.content:
As S/4HANA landscapes grow and user adoption increases, performance issues in custom CDS (Core Data Services) views can emerge in ways that are not immediately obvious.
Two patterns in particular are responsible for a significant share of unexpected CPU spikes in production HANA systems:

  1. CDS views using locale-based text retrieval that silently bypass HANA's most efficient execution engine.
  2. ABAP programs passing large filter lists to CDS views, generating thousands of SQL filter conditions at runtime.

Both patterns are common in custom development, both are easy to miss during development and testing, and both have clean, well-documented solutions. This post walks through each one in detail.

Contents

  1. Step 0 — Diagnose Before You Fix
  2. Fix #1 — CDS Views with Locale Parameters Bypassing the HEX Engine
  3. Fix #2 — Large IN-List Conditions from ABAP Range Tables
  4. General Best Practices
  5. Summary
  6. References

Step 0: Before You Fix — Diagnose First

Before applying any optimization, confirm which queries are causing elevated CPU usage.
The SQL Plan Cache in SAP HANA is the primary tool for this.

Check the Execution Engine of a Specific Query

SELECT EXECUTION_ENGINE, STATEMENT_HASH, STATEMENT_STRING
FROM M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE
WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

The EXECUTION_ENGINE column reveals which engine processed the query:

ValueMeaning
HEXHigh-Performance Analytical Engine — optimal for CDS views
COLUMNColumn Engine — less optimized, higher CPU for CDS workloads
ROWRow Engine — used for row-store tables, not typical for CDS views

If you see COLUMN where you expect HEX, the query is not running at its full potential.

Monitor CPU and Memory at the System Level

SAP HANA Cockpit and HANA Studio both provide dashboards for CPU and memory usage over configurable time windows.
Key indicators to watch:

  • CPU usage spikes above 70–80% during business hours, correlated with Fiori app usage.
  • Out-of-Memory (OOM) dumps in HANA trace files or HANA Cockpit - these indicate memory pressure from specific statements.
  • Statement Memory Limit - if individual SQL statements approach the configured limit, they may be terminated or cause cascading failures.
Tip: Always capture both a 7-day and a 24-hour view to distinguish between sustained load and isolated spikes.
This distinction determines whether a fix is urgent or can be planned during a maintenance window.

CDS Views with Locale Parameters Bypassing the HEX Engine

SAP Note 3513507  ·  SAP Note 3076534

Background: How SAP HANA Handles Multilingual Text

SAP systems are designed to operate in multiple languages.
Many business entities - such as product descriptions, unit of measure texts, and customer category labels - are stored in dedicated text tables: separate database tables containing one row per language per entity.


Entity KeyLanguage KeyDescription
ITEM-001ENSteel Pipe
ITEM-001DEStahlrohr
ITEM-001HEצינור פלדה
ITEM-001JA鋼管

When a user opens a Fiori application with their session language set to Hebrew (he), the system must return the Hebrew text row and render it correctly in right-to-left script.
The same mechanism applies to any language that differs from the system installation default.

What WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) Does

In SAP HANA CDS, text tables are modeled using the @ObjectModel.text.association annotation.
When a Fiori application reads a CDS view backed by such an association, it automatically appends a WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) clause to the generated SQL query, passing the current user's session locale as the parameter value.

SELECT *
FROM MY_CDS_VIEW
WITH PARAMETERS ('LOCALE' = 'he')

This instructs HANA to join the text table and return only the rows matching the Hebrew language key.
Without this parameter, the CDS view would either return texts for all languages or fall back to a system default, neither of which is appropriate for a user-facing Fiori application.

Example — Hebrew locale in action

A product or entity search CDS view with LOCALE = 'he' returns Hebrew descriptions:

KeyDescription
ITEM-001צינור פלדה (Steel Pipe)
ITEM-002ספק ראשי בע"מ (Main Supplier Ltd.)
ITEM-003חברת דוגמה בע"מ (Example Company Ltd.)

The Problem: HEX Engine Limitation

SAP HANA's HEX (High-Performance Analytical Execution) Engine is the modern columnar execution engine optimized for CDS views accessed from Fiori applications.
It delivers substantially lower CPU consumption compared to the fallback Column Engine, particularly for queries involving joins, projections, and filters typical of CDS-based reads.

However, as documented in SAP Note 3513507, the HEX engine has a known limitation: it cannot process queries containing a WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) clause.
When such a query arrives, HANA silently falls back to the Column Engine.

Why this goes undetected: This fallback is entirely invisible during development and unit testing.
Results are correct - just processed less efficiently.
The impact only becomes apparent in production, where many users simultaneously access the same Fiori apps in a non-English locale.

This is particularly relevant in any deployment where the working language differs from the system installation default - including Hebrew, Arabic, Japanese, Chinese, Thai, and others.

The Solution: Force HEX Engine via ANNOTATION Hint

Since the HEX engine bypass is a platform-level limitation rather than a query logic issue, the fix is to explicitly instruct HANA to use HEX via a DB hint applied through HANA's Annotation mechanism (SAP Note 3076534).
This approach requires no changes to the CDS view definition, the ABAP application layer, or any transport.

Step 1

Verify the current execution engine before making any changes:

SELECT EXECUTION_ENGINE, STATEMENT_HASH, STATEMENT_STRING
FROM M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE
WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

Step 2

Apply the HEX hint via the ANNOTATION mechanism:

ANNOTATE "YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME" SET 'HINT' = 'USE_HEX_PLAN';

Step 3

Confirm the annotation is registered:

SELECT * FROM ANNOTATIONS;

Step 4

Clear the SQL Plan Cache so HANA generates a fresh execution plan:

ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE SQL PLAN CACHE
  WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

Step 5

Re-trigger the Fiori app, then verify the engine has changed:

SELECT EXECUTION_ENGINE, STATEMENT_HASH
FROM M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE
WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

The EXECUTION_ENGINE column should now show HEX.

To roll back the hint at any time:

ANNOTATE "YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME" UNSET 'HINT';

Large IN-List Conditions from ABAP Range Tables

SAP Note 2399993 -  SAP HANA Fast Data Access (FDA)

The Problem: How ABAP Generates Thousands of SQL Filter Conditions

A common ABAP pattern for reading data from a CDS view is to build a range table and pass it using the IN operator:

SELECT *
FROM zcds_my_view
INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_results)
WHERE key_field IN @LR_range.

This is clean, readable ABAP - but it has a critical hidden cost at the database layer.
When ABAP's DB interface translates this statement into SQL, it expands the range table into a series of explicit filter conditions:

SELECT * FROM ZCDS_MY_VIEW
WHERE KEY_FIELD = 'VALUE001'
   OR KEY_FIELD = 'VALUE002'
   OR KEY_FIELD = 'VALUE003'
   -- ... potentially thousands more conditions

If lr_range contains hundreds or thousands of entries - entirely realistic for document processing, purchase order workflows, or batch jobs - HANA must parse and compile this statement each time it is executed.
This compilation overhead, not the data retrieval itself, is what drives CPU spikes and degraded response times.

Why this goes undetected: In development and testing, range tables are typically small (a handful of test records).
The problem scales silently with production data volumes, only surfacing once the system is under real load.

This pattern is additionally harmful because:

  • It generates near-identical but technically distinct SQL statements (different values each run), preventing effective plan cache reuse.
  • It worsens over time as data volumes and document counts grow.

The Solution: FOR ALL ENTRIES with HANA FDA Hint

Replace the IN operator with FOR ALL ENTRIES IN, combined with a HANA Fast Data Access (FDA) hint:

SELECT *
  FROM zcds_my_view
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_results)
  FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @LR_range
  WHERE key_field = @LR_range-low
  %_HINTS HDB '&prefer_join_with_fda 1&'.

Why This Works: SAP HANA Fast Data Access (FDA)

FDA is a SAP HANA optimization specifically designed for FOR ALL ENTRIES queries. Instead of expanding the input list into SQL filter conditions, HANA:

  1. Loads the input list into a HANA-managed temporary table in memory.
  2. Joins the CDS view result against this temporary table.
  3. Lets the HANA query optimizer determine the most efficient join strategy.

The result is a single, stable, reusable SQL execution plan regardless of how many entries are in the input list.
Compilation overhead is eliminated, plan cache reuse is maximized, and CPU consumption is substantially reduced.

Comparison: IN vs FOR ALL ENTRIES + FDA

AspectIN '@LR_range'FOR ALL ENTRIES + FDA
SQL generatedOne condition per range entrySingle join against temp table
Compile timeGrows with range sizeConstant
Plan cache reuseLow (unique SQL per execution)High (stable plan)
CPU impact at scaleHighLow
ABAP complexityMinimalSlightly higher

 

Important considerations for FOR ALL ENTRIES: If the input table is empty, FOR ALL ENTRIES returns no rows, unlike IN which would return all rows in that case.
It also implicitly removes duplicate rows from the result set.
Ensure your application logic accounts for both behaviors before applying this change.
 

General Best Practices

Always Test in a Non-Production System First

Both fixes involve changes at the database or ABAP layer that affect execution plans.
Apply in a development or quality system first, execute the affected Fiori apps or programs, verify correct results, and confirm the execution plan has changed as expected via M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE before promoting to production.

Manage the SQL Plan Cache Proactively

After applying any execution hint or changing ABAP logic, clear the relevant SQL Plan Cache entries.
Leaving stale entries in place will delay the effect of any optimization until the cache entry naturally expires.

-- Clear cache entries for a specific CDS view
ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE SQL PLAN CACHE
  WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

-- Clear cache entries for a specific statement hash
ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE SQL PLAN CACHE
  WHERE STATEMENT_HASH = 'your_statement_hash_here';

Monitor Regularly, Not Just Reactively

Set up routine monitoring for:

  • CPU usage trends over 7-day rolling windows - distinguishes growing load from isolated incidents.
  • Top SQL statements by CPU time via M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE or HANA Cockpit's SQL Monitor.
  • Out-of-Memory dumps - a leading indicator of memory pressure before they become business-impacting outages.

Early detection allows you to address performance issues during planned maintenance windows rather than under incident pressure.

Summary

Both issues share a common theme: code that looks correct and performs acceptably in development causes measurable production impact at scale.
The fixes are targeted, low-risk, and reversible.

IssueRoot CauseSolutionSAP Note
High CPU on CDS views with localeWITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) prevents HEX engine usageApply USE_HEX_PLAN hint via ANNOTATION3513507, 3076534
High CPU from large filter listsABAP IN operator generates thousands of SQL conditionsUse FOR ALL ENTRIES with FDA hint2399993

 

 

References

  • SAP Note 3513507 — HANA HEX Engine not used for queries containing WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?)
  • SAP Note 3076534 — Temporary use of ANNOTATION HINT
  • SAP Note 2399993 — FAQ: SAP HANA Fast Data Access (FDA)
  • SAP Help Portal — Accessing Localized Texts in CDS Views
  • SAP HANA Administration Guide — SQL Plan Cache

entry.title: Re: Improving SAP HANA DB Performance in S/4HANA: Two Common CDS View Pitfalls and How to Fix Them
entry.content: The HEX fallback issue highlights a deeper challenge in SAP HANA abstraction layers. Developers assume CDS equals optimized execution, but hidden clauses like locale parameters silently change the execution path. This reinforces the need for plan cache validation as a standard development discipline, not a reactive troubleshooting step.

Instead of focusing on features, it reflects how operations teams actually experience system health across multiple dimensions in day-to-day SAP environments.

entry.title: Re: ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code

Being able to create, activate, and manage ABAP objects directly from VS Code reduces context switching significantly, which is often one of the biggest productivity drains in enterprise development.

entry.title: Re: stock removal not happening while doing MIGO because...
entry.content: Existing stock without batch cannot be consumed once batch management is active. You must clear the old non-batch stock and repost it with batch information. Going forward, always activate batch management before the first goods receipt to avoid mismatches.

entry.title: No Event Mesh? No Problem — Integrating Service Orders to FSM via CPI Polling
entry.content:
SAP Field Service Management (FSM) relies on timely and accurate service order data from SAP S/4HANA Cloud to dispatch technicians, schedule work, and close jobs efficiently. The standard integration between these two systems leverages SAP Event Mesh — a powerful, cloud-native messaging backbone on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) — to stream real-time service order lifecycle events into FSM.

However, not every customer has an active SAP Event Mesh subscription on BTP. This could be due to licensing constraints, cost considerations, or an IT strategy that minimises the number of BTP service subscriptions. For these customers, the standard event-driven integration path hits a wall before it even starts.

In this blog, I will walk through a practical workaround: a custom SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) polling iFlow that periodically queries the S/4HANA Cloud Service Order OData API, formats the result as a synthetic Event Mesh payload, and hands it off to the standard "Replicate Service Order from SAP S/4HANA Cloud to SAP FSM" iFlow — no Event Mesh subscription required.

Note: This work will have some limitations and considersations before adopting.

How the Standard Integration Works

The standard SAP-delivered integration uses communication arrangement SAP_COM_0092 on the S/4HANA Cloud side to publish business events to SAP Event Mesh. A subscriber iFlow on CPI then picks up those events and replicates the affected service orders into FSM.

The four events subscribed to in the standard scenario are:

  • sap/s4/beh/serviceorder/v1/ServiceOrder/Released/v1
  • sap/s4/beh/serviceorder/v1/ServiceOrder/Completed/v1
  • sap/s4/beh/serviceorder/v1/ServiceOrder/ReleaseRevoked/v1
  • sap/s4/beh/serviceorder/v1/ServiceOrder/ChgdWhenReleased/v1

Each event carries a minimal payload (service order ID, UUID, type, and description). The CPI iFlow then fetches the full service order details and pushes them into FSM. This architecture is clean, near-real-time, and fully supported by SAP — but it has one hard prerequisite: SAP Event Mesh must be provisioned and configured on BTP.

The Challenge: Event Mesh as a Hard Prerequisite

For customers who have already established an S/4HANA Cloud-to-FSM integration but do not have an Event Mesh subscription, the above architecture creates a blocking dependency. Specifically:

  1. SAP Event Mesh is a paid, separately subscribed BTP service. Customers who have not planned for it may not have it available.
  2. Activating Event Mesh mid-project introduces procurement, provisioning, and configuration lead time — often weeks — that can delay go-live.
  3. Some customers deliberately keep their BTP footprint minimal. Adding Event Mesh solely for service order integration conflicts with their platform governance model.
  4. In brownfield or upgrade scenarios, re-configuring communication arrangements and queue subscriptions can introduce regression risk to existing integrations.

These constraints call for an alternative approach — one that achieves the same functional outcome (service order replication into FSM) without any dependency on SAP Event Mesh.

Workaround approach: CPI Polling iFlow

The workaround introduces a lightweight custom polling iFlow in CPI that mimics the role of SAP Event Mesh — without actually using it. The iFlow runs on a scheduled timer (for example, every 10 minutes), queries the S/4HANA Cloud Service Order OData API for records changed since the last run, constructs a synthetic Event Mesh-style JSON payload for each changed order, and invokes the standard "Replicate Service Order" iFlow via HTTPS — exactly as if the message had arrived from Event Mesh.

Crucially, the standard iFlow is left completely untouched. This ensures SAP support scope is preserved and future upgrades to the standard content remain non-breaking.

Layer

Component

Remark

Source

S/4HANA Cloud – API_SERVICE_ORDER_SRV OData API

Standard API, no change needed

Custom iFlow

CPI Polling iFlow (customer-built)

Scheduled every ~10 min

Payload Builder

Constructs synthetic EMS JSON message

Mimics Event Mesh envelope

Target

Standard CPI iFlow – Replicate Service Order

Called via HTTPS, unmodified

Destination

SAP FSM

Unchanged end target

 

Step 1: Querying Changed Service Orders

The polling iFlow calls the standard OData API below, substituting the timestamp from the last successful run (stored as a CPI local variable):

GET /sap/opu/odata/sap/API_SERVICE_ORDER_SRV/A_ServiceOrder
  ?$select=ServiceOrder,ServiceObjectType,ServiceOrderUUID,ServiceOrderDescription,ServiceOrderType,ServiceDocChangedDateTime,ServiceOrderIsReleased,ServiceOrderIsCompleted,ServiceOrderIsRejected
  &$filter=(
    ServiceDocChangedDateTime gt datetimeoffset'<LAST_RUN_TIMESTAMP>'
    and (ServiceOrderType eq 'SVO1' or ServiceOrderType eq 'SVO2'
         or ServiceOrderType eq 'RPO1' or ServiceOrderType eq 'RPO2')
    and (ServiceOrderIsReleased eq 'X'
         or ServiceOrderIsCompleted eq 'X'
         or ServiceOrderIsRejected eq 'X')
  )

The filter targets only the service order types that are relevant for FSM replication (SVO1, SVO2, RPO1, RPO2) and only records that are in a terminal or active business state (Released, Completed, or Rejected). This keeps the result set lean and avoids processing draft or in-progress orders that FSM does not yet need.

The last-run timestamp is read from a CPI local variable at the start of each execution and updated to the current timestamp at the end of a successful run. This creates a sliding window of changes and avoids re-processing already-replicated orders.

Step 2: Splitting and Iterating

The OData response may return multiple service orders in a single call. The iFlow uses a Splitter step to process each service order independently. This is important because:

  • A failure for one service order does not block replication of others.
  • Error handling and retry logic can be applied at the individual record level.
  • The standard iFlow expects a single service order event per invocation, matching the one-event-per-message model of SAP Event Mesh.

Step 3: Constructing the Synthetic Event Mesh Payload

For each service order, the iFlow builds a JSON payload that is structurally identical to what the standard iFlow would receive from SAP Event Mesh. The event type in the payload is determined by the status flags returned by the OData API:

  • ServiceOrderIsReleased = 'X'  →  event type: ServiceOrder.Released.v1
  • ServiceOrderIsCompleted = 'X'  →  event type: ServiceOrder.Completed.v1
  • ServiceOrderIsRejected = 'X'  →  event type: ServiceOrder.ReleaseRevoked.v1

A sample constructed payload looks like this:

{
  "type": "sap.s4.beh.serviceorder.v1.ServiceOrder.Released.v1",
  "specversion": "1.0",
  "source": "/default/sap.s4.beh/0LEUVB6",
  "id": "42010aef-4c3b-1fe1-8eaa-82de726150ac",
  "time": "2026-04-16T05:21:15Z",
  "datacontenttype": "application/json",
  "data": {
    "ServiceOrder": "8000000176",
    "CustMgmtObjectType": "BUS2000116",
    "ServiceOrderUUID": "42010aef-4c8d-1fd1-8de5-b5128633bb6f",
    "ServiceOrderDescription": "Annual maintenance visit",
    "ServiceOrderType": "SVO1"
  }
}

The id field can be populated using the ServiceOrderUUID, and the time field is set to the current execution timestamp. The source field should match the BTP source identifier configured in the target standard iFlow — check the iFlow's configuration parameters to confirm the correct value.

Step 4: Invoking the Standard iFlow via HTTPS

Rather than using a ProcessDirect channel (which would require modifying the standard iFlow to expose an internal endpoint), the custom iFlow calls the standard iFlow's public HTTPS endpoint. The technical user performing this call must be assigned the WorkspaceArtifactsDeploy role in CPI to have permission to trigger iFlow executions.

This design keeps a clean boundary: the standard iFlow remains a black box from the custom iFlow's perspective. If SAP updates the standard content in a future release, no changes are needed in the custom polling iFlow as long as the input message contract (the EMS JSON structure) remains stable.

Here is a crisp, highly structured version of your content, optimized for quick scanning and professional review.

⚠️ Limitations & Considerations: Polling Workaround

While this polling workaround is a pragmatic alternative, it lacks the native capabilities of an event-driven architecture. Review these 7 critical limitations before deploying to production.

1. Polling Latency (Near-Real-Time, Not Real-Time)

  • The Gap: Scheduled runs (e.g., every 10 mins) introduce a maximum data lag equal to the polling interval, whereas an event-driven model pushes changes in seconds.

  • Impact: Field techs may act on stale, modified, or canceled service order data on their mobile apps—a high risk for emergency work orders.

  • Mitigation: Shorten the polling interval during peak hours, balancing against S/4HANA Cloud API rate limits.

2. Snapshot-Only (No Intermediate States)

  • The Gap: Polling only captures a snapshot of the latest state. If an order quickly transitions through multiple statuses between runs (e.g., Released $\rightarrow$ Modified $\rightarrow$ Completed), intermediate states are lost.

  • Impact: FSM business rules triggered by specific state transitions (like ChgdWhenReleased or parts checks) will be skipped, causing workflow inconsistencies.

  • Mitigation: Redesign FSM business rules (if any) to trigger actions based on the final current state rather than historical transitions.

3. Missing ReleaseRevoked Context

  • The Gap: The standard integration publishes a distinct ReleaseRevoked event when a released order is recalled. Polling only detects a raw "Rejected/Revoked" status and cannot determine the historical sequence.

  • Impact: SLA timers or automations meant to cancel upon a revoke action may fail to trigger if the release and revoke both happen within a single polling window.

4. Increased S/4HANA Cloud API Load

  • The Gap: Unlike push events, polling repeatedly queries S/4HANA OData APIs on a fixed schedule, even when no data has changed.

  • Impact: Unnecessary network traffic and rapid consumption of your S/4HANA API license quotas.

  • Mitigation: Implement an adaptive schedule (e.g., poll less frequently overnight/weekends) and routinely monitor API usage logs.

5. Loss of ChgdWhenReleased Granularity

  • The Gap: The standard event model sends precise, field-level delta triggers (e.g., only the schedule time changed). Polling can only see that a change occurred, forcing a full-record refresh to FSM.

  • Impact: FSM automations that depend on specific field triggers (like sending an SMS to a tech only if the priority escalates) cannot be targeted cleanly. FSM will only see a bulk data overwrite.

6. Timestamp-Based Edge Cases

  • The Gap: Delta detection relies strictly on filtering the ServiceDocChangedDateTime field.

  • Impact: Clock drift between CPI and S/4HANA can cause skipped or double-processed records.

    If the CPI local variable storing the Last Run Timestamp is wiped (via redeployment or restart), data will be missed without a manual reset. Boundary records matching the exact timestamp filter might be dropped.

  • Mitigation: Build a 1–2 minute overlap window into your timestamp logic, and set up a monitoring alert for missing or stale timestamp variables.

Conclusion

The absence of SAP Event Mesh is not a roadblock for integrating S/4HANA Cloud service orders with SAP FSM. A custom CPI polling iFlow that mimics an Event Mesh payload offers a viable, non-invasive workaround. By keeping the standard "Replicate Service Order" iFlow completely unmodified, this conservative approach isolates the custom logic into a single, easily maintainable layer.

However, teams must accept the trade-offs: near-real-time latency, the loss of intermediate state history, and added operational complexity around error handling and timestamp management. With a robust retry mechanism and proper monitoring, this architecture can reliably support production workloads.

entry.title: Re: alternative to transaction KE5Y
entry.content: Start with Profit Centers – Actuals (F0944A) for operational reporting and Profit Centers – Plan/Actual/Variance for management reporting. Combine with Analysis for Office if your users want Excel‑style flexibility.

entry.title: Expired Useful Life” and “Expired Periods”
entry.content:
We have observed that the fields “Expired Useful Life” and “Expired Periods” are not displayed in the asset master for asset 280036, as the asset was capitalized in the current fiscal year.

However, for asset 280033, these fields are visible since the asset capitalization occurred in prior fiscal years, where depreciation periods have already elapsed.

Just wondering: I'm used to create my lcl_app class at the beginning, I instanciate it in the initialization with data(app) = new cl_app() and in the start-of-selection I just call my app->run (or main, or whatever name you want to call it)

entry.title: Maintain another exchange rate type different from type M for the Monitor Purchase Order Items app
entry.content:
I've tried to maintain another exchange rate type to display data for Monitor Purchase Order Items app. I found SAP Note
--- Steps to Reproduce ---

App: Monitor Purchase Order Items


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Maintain another exchange rate type different from ty...
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you for your patience while I looked into your question.

I can confirm that the behavior you observed is standard in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

As outlined in SAP Note 3681306, the Monitor Purchase Order Items app uses exchange rate type “M” for currency conversion in purchasing analytics. The exchange rate is derived from the maintained currency exchange rates based on the Purchase Order date. Even if a different or fixed exchange rate is maintained at the purchase order level, it is not considered by this analytical app.

At present, there is no configuration available to change the exchange rate type used in this app. Therefore, the system will continue to use exchange rate type M by design.

If your business requires reporting based on a different exchange rate type,you may consider submitting an enhancement request via the SAP Customer Influence program for Product Management review in future releases.

I hope this helps clarify the behavior. Please feel free to reach out if you have any further questions—I’ll be happy to support you further.

Best Regards

Aishwarya

entry.title: Sales order plant change when order created via IDOC
entry.content:
Hello experts,

In S4HANA ON PREMISE 2023, I have a scenario where sales orders are created via IDOCs sent from an external system. In WEDI partner profile, the process code of the message type indicates IDOC_INPUT_ORDERS as the identification of process.

My requirement is to modify the line-item plant before save under certain circumstances. So, I created a logic in USEREXIT_SAVE_DOCUMENT_PREPARE but when the order is saved the default plant is not changes although the execution passes from the plant re-determination code.

If I create the order via VA01 then the plant is re-determined as expected and the execution flow is the same with IDOC. 

So, it seems that another standard EDI processing step runs after the exit and overwrites the plant.

How can I verify this?

Regards,

Konstantinos
